questions.title: Conditional probability definition
questions.prompt: Define conditional probability P(A | B). State Bayes' theorem. What does it mean for two events A and B to be independent?

markscheme.text: P(A | B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B), defined when P(B) > 0
Bayes' theorem: P(A | B) = P(B | A) · P(A) / P(B)
A and B are independent if P(A ∩ B) = P(A) · P(B), equivalently P(A | B) = P(A) (knowledge of B gives no information about A)

questions.title: Law of total probability
questions.prompt: State the Law of Total Probability. Explain when it is useful and give a medical testing example: a disease affects 1% of the population. A test is 90% sensitive (P(positive | disease) = 0.9) and 95% specific (P(negative | no disease) = 0.95). Calculate the probability that a randomly chosen person tests positive.

markscheme.text: Law of total probability: if B₁,...,Bₙ partition the sample space, then P(A) = Σ P(A | Bᵢ)·P(Bᵢ)
P(positive) = P(positive | disease)·P(disease) + P(positive | no disease)·P(no disease)

questions.title: Applying Bayes' theorem
questions.prompt: Using the medical testing scenario from above (disease prevalence 1%, sensitivity 90%, specificity 95%), calculate the probability that a person has the disease given that they tested positive. Comment on this result — why might it surprise a doctor?
questions.hint: Apply Bayes' theorem with the prior P(disease) = 0.01 and the likelihood P(positive | disease) = 0.9.

markscheme.text: P(disease | positive) = P(positive | disease) · P(disease) / P(positive)
Denominator (from previous question): 0.0585
P(disease | positive) = 0.009 / 0.0585 ≈ 0.154 (about 15%)

Interpretation: despite a 90% sensitive test, a positive result only means ~15% chance of having the disease. This is because the disease is rare (low prior), so most positives are false positives — known as the base rate fallacy

questions.title: Monty Hall and conditional independence
questions.prompt:
(i) In the Monty Hall problem, a contestant chooses door 1. The host (who knows where the car is) opens door 3 to reveal a goat. Should the contestant switch to door 2? Prove your answer using Bayes' theorem.
(ii) Define conditional independence: A and B are conditionally independent given C. Give an example where two events are conditionally independent given a third but not marginally independent.
questions.hint: For (i), let Cᵢ be the event 'car is behind door i'. Compute P(C₁ | host opens 3) and P(C₂ | host opens 3).

markscheme.text: Let Cᵢ = car behind door i, H₃ = host opens door 3. Priors: P(C₁)=P(C₂)=P(C₃)=1/3
P(H₃ | C₁) = 1/2 (host can open 2 or 3); P(H₃ | C₂) = 1 (must open 3 as door 1 chosen and car at 2 ≠ 3); P(H₃ | C₃) = 0
Conditional independence: A ⊥ B | C iff P(A ∩ B | C) = P(A|C)·P(B|C)

Example: let C = 'it is raining'; A = 'road is wet'; B = 'umbrella is used'. A and B are correlated (both caused by rain), so not marginally independent. But given C, knowing A gives no additional info about B (they share only the common cause C) — conditionally independent given C

questions.title: Bayesian inference and sequential updating
questions.prompt: This question concerns Bayesian probability.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: You have two coins: coin A is fair (P(heads)=0.5) and coin B is biased (P(heads)=0.8). You pick a coin at random (each with probability 0.5) and flip it twice, obtaining HH. Calculate the posterior probability that you chose coin A.

markscheme.text: Prior: P(A)=P(B)=0.5. Likelihood: P(HH|A)=0.25, P(HH|B)=0.64

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Using P(A|HH) ≈ 0.281 as your new prior, you flip the same coin again and observe T. Update the posterior to find P(A | HHT). Show your work.

markscheme.text: New prior: P(A)=0.281, P(B)=0.719. Likelihood: P(T|A)=0.5, P(T|B)=0.2

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain the concepts of prior, likelihood, posterior, and evidence in Bayesian inference. How does the posterior become the prior for the next observation (sequential updating)?

markscheme.text: Prior P(H): your belief about hypothesis H before seeing data
Likelihood P(D|H): how probable the observed data D is under hypothesis H
Evidence P(D): total probability of data under all hypotheses (normalising constant)
Posterior P(H|D) ∝ P(D|H)·P(H). For sequential data: posterior after observing D₁ becomes the prior for D₂; equivalent to processing all data at once (multiplication of likelihoods)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Three machines A, B, C produce 20%, 30%, 50% of a factory's output respectively. The defect rates are 5%, 3%, 2%. A defective item is found. What is the probability it came from machine A?

markscheme.text: P(A | defective) = P(defective|A)·P(A)/P(defective) = 0.01/0.029

questions.title: Spam filtering and naive Bayes
questions.prompt: This question concerns Bayesian classification.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the naïve Bayes classifier for spam filtering. What is the 'naïve' assumption, and when is it reasonable?

markscheme.text: Model: classify email as spam/ham based on word frequencies. P(spam|words) ∝ P(spam)·∏ P(wᵢ|spam)
Naïve assumption: words are conditionally independent given the class label (given spam, occurrence of 'free' doesn't affect probability of 'win')
Reasonable when: dependencies between words are weak or cancel out in practice; often works well empirically despite the assumption being false

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: You have a training set where 40% of emails are spam. In spam emails, 'free' appears in 60% and 'meeting' in 5%. In ham, 'free' appears in 10% and 'meeting' in 40%. A new email contains both 'free' and 'meeting'. Classify it using naïve Bayes. Show all working.

markscheme.text: P(spam) = 0.4, P(ham) = 0.6
P(free, meeting | spam) = 0.6 × 0.05 = 0.03 (naïve assumption); P(free, meeting | ham) = 0.1 × 0.4 = 0.04
Score(ham) > Score(spam) → classify as ham. P(spam | email) = 0.012/(0.012+0.024) = 1/3 ≈ 33%

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What problem arises in naïve Bayes if a word never appears in the training set for one class? Describe the Laplace (add-one) smoothing solution.

markscheme.text: Problem: if P(w|spam) = 0 (word never seen in spam training), the entire product becomes 0 — the classifier ignores all other evidence
Laplace smoothing: add a pseudo-count of 1 to every word count. P(w|class) = (count(w, class) + 1) / (total words in class + |vocabulary|)
Effect: no probability is zero; rare words get a small positive probability; classifier remains robust to unseen words

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: In practice, naïve Bayes multiplies many small probabilities, causing numerical underflow. How is this addressed?

markscheme.text: Work in log-space: compute log P(class) + Σ log P(wᵢ|class). Compare log-scores rather than probabilities
Avoids multiplying many small numbers; additions are numerically stable; final classification based on which class has the higher log-score

questions.title: Discrete random variable basics
questions.prompt: Define: (i) a discrete random variable X; (ii) its probability mass function (PMF); (iii) the expected value E[X]. State the condition that a PMF must satisfy.

markscheme.text: Discrete RV: a function X: Ω → ℝ where Ω is a sample space; X takes at most countably many values
PMF: p(x) = P(X = x) for each value x. Conditions: p(x) ≥ 0 for all x, and Σ_x p(x) = 1
Expected value: E[X] = Σ_x x · p(x) (sum over all values weighted by probability)

questions.title: Geometric distribution and memorylessness
questions.prompt: The geometric distribution models the number of trials until the first success (with success probability p). State its PMF, expected value, and the memoryless property. Verify the memoryless property formally.

markscheme.text: PMF: P(X = k) = (1−p)^(k−1) · p for k = 1, 2, 3, ... (number of trials until first success)
Memoryless property: P(X > m+n | X > m) = P(X > n) — given that the first success hasn't occurred in m trials, the distribution of remaining trials is the same as the original
Verification: P(X > m) = (1−p)^m. P(X > m+n | X > m) = P(X > m+n)/P(X > m) = (1−p)^(m+n)/(1−p)^m = (1−p)^n = P(X > n) ✓

questions.title: Expectation by indicator variables
questions.prompt: A fair coin is flipped n times. Let X be the number of heads. Using indicator random variables, derive E[X]. Then generalise: a biased coin with P(heads) = p is flipped n times. What is E[X]? Now, in a group of 30 people, what is the expected number of pairs who share a birthday? (Assume 365 days in a year.)
questions.hint: For the birthday problem, define an indicator variable for each pair. There are C(30,2) pairs.

markscheme.text: Let Iᵢ = 1 if flip i is heads. E[Iᵢ] = P(heads) = 1/2. X = I₁+...+Iₙ; E[X] = n·(1/2) = n/2 by linearity of expectation
Biased coin: E[X] = n·p (same argument)
Birthday problem: define Iᵢⱼ = 1 if persons i and j share a birthday. P(same birthday) = 1/365
Number of pairs = C(30,2) = 435; E[shared birthday pairs] = 435 × 1/365 ≈ 1.19

questions.title: Functions of random variables and LOTUS
questions.prompt:
(i) State the Law of the Unconscious Statistician (LOTUS) for discrete random variables.
(ii) A fair die has faces {1,2,3,4,5,6}. Let X be the result of one roll. Find E[X], E[X²], and E[(X−E[X])²] (the variance).
(iii) You win £X² for rolling X. Is this a fair game at a cost of £15 per roll? Justify.
questions.hint: Use LOTUS for E[X²]: E[g(X)] = Σ g(x)·P(X=x).

markscheme.text: LOTUS: for g: ℝ→ℝ, E[g(X)] = Σ_x g(x)·P(X=x)
Expected winnings per roll = E[X²] = 91/6 ≈ 15.17 > 15. Yes, slightly favourable at £15 cost (expected profit ≈ 17p per roll)

questions.title: Coupon collector and expected waiting times
questions.prompt: This question concerns the Coupon Collector Problem.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: There are n types of coupon; each purchase gives a uniformly random coupon type. Let T be the total number of purchases to collect all n types. Express T = T₁ + T₂ + ... + Tₙ where Tᵢ is the number of purchases to get the i-th new type after already having i−1 distinct types. State the distribution of Tᵢ and find E[Tᵢ].

markscheme.text: After i−1 distinct coupons, probability of getting a new one is p = (n−i+1)/n. Each trial is independent.
Tᵢ ~ Geometric((n−i+1)/n)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Using part (a) and linearity of expectation, derive E[T] in terms of n and the harmonic number Hₙ = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ... + 1/n. Evaluate for n=4.

markscheme.text: E[T] = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ E[Tᵢ] = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ n/(n−i+1) = n·Σⱼ₌₁ⁿ 1/j = n·Hₙ (substituting j = n−i+1)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Since Hₙ ≈ ln n for large n, E[T] ≈ n ln n. Explain intuitively why the last few coupons take so long to collect. Compare this to the O(n log n) complexity of comparison sorting — is the connection a coincidence?

markscheme.text: The last coupon: probability of getting the final missing type is 1/n, so expected wait is n trials. The last few distinct types take O(n) each → they dominate the sum

E[T] = n·Hₙ ≈ n ln n. The O(n log n) lower bound for comparison sorting also arises from a counting argument (n! permutations → log₂(n!) ≈ n log₂ n). Both are instances of the same information-theoretic structure
Not a coincidence: both problems require 'distinguishing' n objects; the total number of bits of information needed is Θ(n log n) in both cases

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Let X be the number of coupons until the first duplicate. Show that E[X] = Θ(√n). (Hint: use the birthday paradox — after k draws, what is the probability of no duplicate?)

markscheme.text: P(no duplicate in k draws) = 1 × (n−1)/n × (n−2)/n × ... × (n−k+1)/n = Π_{i=0}^{k-1}(1 − i/n)
Using 1−x ≈ e^(−x): product ≈ e^(−k(k−1)/(2n)). This ≈ 1/2 when k(k−1)/2 ≈ n·ln 2, i.e. k ≈ √(2n·ln 2) = Θ(√n)
E[X] ≈ Σ_{k=0}^{∞} P(no duplicate in k draws) ≈ Σ e^{−k²/(2n)} ≈ √(πn/2) = Θ(√n)

questions.title: Binomial distribution and applications
questions.prompt: This question concerns the Binomial distribution.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define the Binomial distribution B(n, p). Derive its PMF and state its expected value and variance.

markscheme.text: B(n,p): number of successes in n independent Bernoulli(p) trials. PMF: P(X=k) = C(n,k)·p^k·(1−p)^(n−k) for k=0,...,n
Derivation: C(n,k) ways to choose which k trials succeed; each such outcome has probability p^k·(1−p)^(n−k)
E[X] = np; Var(X) = np(1−p) (both derived by indicator variable decomposition X = Σ Iᵢ)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A network packet is independently dropped with probability p = 0.01 at each of n = 10 routers. Let X be the number of drops. Find P(X ≥ 2). Would you expect the Poisson approximation to be accurate here? Justify.

markscheme.text: P(X≥2) ≈ 1 − 0.9044 − 0.0914 = 0.0042. Poisson(λ=0.1) would give P(X≥2)=1−e^{−0.1}−0.1e^{−0.1}≈0.0047; reasonable approximation (n large, p small, λ=np=0.1 moderate)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: State the Poisson distribution Poisson(λ). Prove that it arises as the limit of B(n, λ/n) as n→∞ with λ fixed.

markscheme.text: Poisson(λ): P(X=k) = e^{−λ}·λ^k/k! for k=0,1,2,...; E[X]=Var(X)=λ
Starting from B(n, λ/n): P(X=k) = C(n,k)·(λ/n)^k·(1−λ/n)^{n−k}
So P(X=k) → (1·λ^k/k!)·e^{−λ}·1 = e^{−λ}λ^k/k! as required

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: The sum of independent Poisson random variables is Poisson. If X ~ Poisson(λ₁) and Y ~ Poisson(λ₂) are independent, state the distribution of X + Y and compute E[X+Y] and Var(X+Y).

markscheme.text: X+Y ~ Poisson(λ₁+λ₂)
E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] = λ₁+λ₂ (linearity of expectation)
Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) = λ₁+λ₂ (since X and Y are independent)

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Variance and standard deviation
questions.prompt: Define the variance Var(X) of a random variable X. State the alternative formula Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])². What is the standard deviation? For a Bernoulli(p) random variable, compute E[X], E[X²], and Var(X).

markscheme.text: Var(X) = E[(X − E[X])²]; standard deviation = √Var(X). Alternative: Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])²
Bernoulli(p): P(X=1)=p, P(X=0)=1−p. E[X] = p
E[X²] = 1²·p + 0²·(1−p) = p. Var(X) = p − p² = p(1−p)

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Linearity of expectation and variance rules
questions.prompt: State: (i) linearity of expectation; (ii) the formula for Var(aX + b); (iii) the formula for Var(X + Y) when X and Y are independent. Prove (iii).

markscheme.text: Linearity: E[aX+b] = a·E[X]+b; E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] (always, no independence needed)
Var(aX+b) = a²·Var(X) (constants shift mean but don't change spread; squaring makes scale factor a²)
Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) when independent (Cov(X,Y)=0)
Proof: Var(X+Y) = E[(X+Y)²]−(E[X+Y])² = E[X²]+2E[XY]+E[Y²]−(E[X])²−2E[X]E[Y]−(E[Y])². Since independent: E[XY]=E[X]E[Y], so 2E[XY]−2E[X]E[Y]=0. Remaining: Var(X)+Var(Y)

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Markov and Chebyshev inequalities
questions.prompt:
(i) State Markov's inequality and Chebyshev's inequality.
(ii) A random variable X has E[X]=50 and Var(X)=25. Use Chebyshev's inequality to bound P(|X−50|≥15).
(iii) Compare this bound to the exact value if X ~ Binomial(100, 0.5). Is Chebyshev tight here?
questions.hint: Chebyshev: P(|X−μ|≥k) ≤ Var(X)/k² for any k>0.

markscheme.text: Markov: for non-negative X, P(X≥a) ≤ E[X]/a. Chebyshev: P(|X−μ|≥k) ≤ σ²/k² where σ²=Var(X)
X~B(100,0.5): E[X]=50, Var(X)=25. P(|X−50|≥15) = P(X≤35 or X≥65) is very small (≈0.003 by normal approximation or exact binomial — much smaller than 1/9)
Chebyshev is very loose here; it's distribution-agnostic so gives a weak bound. For specific distributions, tighter bounds are available

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Generating functions
questions.prompt:
(i) Define the probability generating function (PGF) G_X(z) of a discrete non-negative integer-valued RV X.
(ii) Show how E[X] and Var(X) can be extracted from G_X.
(iii) Find the PGF of a Poisson(λ) random variable and use it to compute E[X] and Var(X).
questions.hint: Differentiate the PGF at z=1. For Var(X), use G''(1)+G'(1)−(G'(1))².

markscheme.text: PGF: G_X(z) = E[z^X] = Σ_{k=0}^∞ P(X=k)·z^k
E[X] = G'_X(1) (differentiate and set z=1); E[X(X−1)] = G''_X(1); Var(X) = G''(1)+G'(1)−(G'(1))²
Poisson(λ): G_X(z) = Σ e^{−λ}λ^k z^k/k! = e^{−λ}·e^{λz} = e^{λ(z−1)}
G''(z) = λ²e^{λ(z−1)}; G''(1) = λ²; Var(X) = λ²+λ−λ² = λ ✓

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Central Limit Theorem and random walks
questions.prompt: This question concerns sums of random variables.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: State the Central Limit Theorem (CLT). A fair coin is flipped n=900 times; let X be the number of heads. Use the CLT to approximate P(435 ≤ X ≤ 465).

markscheme.text: CLT: if X₁,...,Xₙ are i.i.d. with mean μ and variance σ², then (X̄ − μ)/(σ/√n) → N(0,1) in distribution as n→∞; equivalently Sₙ = Σ Xᵢ ≈ N(nμ, nσ²)
P(−1≤Z≤1) ≈ 0.683 (68.3% rule for normal distribution)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A symmetric random walk on ℤ starts at 0. At each step, move +1 with probability 1/2 and −1 with probability 1/2. Let Sₙ be the position after n steps. Find E[Sₙ] and Var(Sₙ).

markscheme.text: Write Sₙ = X₁+...+Xₙ where Xᵢ = ±1 with probability 1/2 each. E[Xᵢ]=0, Var(Xᵢ)=1
E[Sₙ] = n·E[X₁] = 0 (by linearity)
Var(Sₙ) = n·Var(X₁) = n (by independence). So std dev = √n — random walk spreads as √n

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain what 'recurrence' means for a random walk. State whether the symmetric 1D random walk is recurrent. How does this change in 2D and 3D?

markscheme.text: Recurrent: the walk returns to its starting position with probability 1 (infinitely often)
1D symmetric random walk: recurrent — by the CLT, the walk visits every integer infinitely often almost surely
2D: also recurrent (Pólya 1921 — probability of return to origin is 1)
3D: transient — probability of return to origin < 1 (≈0.34). The phrase: 'a drunk man will find his way home, but a drunk bird may get lost forever'

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: State Jensen's inequality. Use it to show that E[√X] ≤ √E[X] for any non-negative random variable X. Give a concrete numerical example.

markscheme.text: Jensen's inequality: for a convex function f, E[f(X)] ≥ f(E[X]). For concave f, the inequality reverses
f(x) = √x is concave (f''(x) < 0). By Jensen: E[√X] ≤ √(E[X])
Example: X takes values 1 and 9 each with prob 1/2. E[X]=5. E[√X]=(1+3)/2=2. √(E[X])=√5≈2.24. Indeed 2 ≤ 2.24 ✓

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Negative binomial and conditional expectation
questions.prompt: This question concerns counting distributions and conditional expectation.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define the Negative Binomial distribution NB(r, p). State its PMF, mean, and variance. Explain the relationship between NB(1, p) and the Geometric distribution.

markscheme.text: NB(r,p): number of failures before the r-th success in a sequence of Bernoulli(p) trials. PMF: P(X=k) = C(k+r−1, k)·(1−p)^k·p^r for k=0,1,2,...
E[X] = r(1−p)/p; Var(X) = r(1−p)/p²
NB(1,p): number of failures before first success = Geometric(p) − 1 (where Geometric counts total trials). Same memoryless structure

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Define conditional expectation E[X|Y=y]. State the tower property (law of total expectation). Use it to find E[X] where X|N ~ Binomial(N, p) and N ~ Poisson(λ).

markscheme.text: E[X|Y=y] = Σ_x x·P(X=x|Y=y); tower property: E[X] = E[E[X|Y]] = Σ_y E[X|Y=y]·P(Y=y)
E[X|N] = N·p (mean of Binomial(N,p))
E[X] = E[E[X|N]] = E[N·p] = p·E[N] = pλ

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Find Var(X) for the same compound distribution X|N~Bin(N,p), N~Poisson(λ). Use the law of total variance: Var(X) = E[Var(X|N)] + Var(E[X|N]).

markscheme.text: Var(X|N) = N·p(1−p); E[Var(X|N)] = E[N]·p(1−p) = λp(1−p)
E[X|N] = Np; Var(E[X|N]) = Var(Np) = p²·Var(N) = p²·λ
Var(X) = λp(1−p) + λp² = λp(1−p+p) = λp. (Note: X ~ Poisson(λp) by compound Poisson result)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: State the weak law of large numbers. Prove it using Chebyshev's inequality.

markscheme.text: WLLN: if X₁,...,Xₙ are i.i.d. with mean μ and finite variance σ², then for any ε>0: P(|X̄ₙ − μ| ≥ ε) → 0 as n→∞
Proof: E[X̄ₙ] = μ; Var(X̄ₙ) = σ²/n
By Chebyshev: P(|X̄ₙ−μ|≥ε) ≤ Var(X̄ₙ)/ε² = σ²/(nε²) → 0 as n→∞ ✓

questions.title: Distribution identification
questions.prompt:
For each scenario, name the most appropriate discrete distribution and state its key parameter(s):
(i) Number of phone calls arriving at a switchboard in a 1-hour period, given calls arrive at a constant average rate of 12 per hour.
(ii) Number of fair coin flips until the first head.
(iii) A batch of 20 components contains 4 defective. You pick 5 at random without replacement. Number of defectives chosen.
(iv) Number of failures before achieving the 3rd success in repeated Bernoulli trials with success probability 0.3.

markscheme.text: (i) Poisson(λ=12) — events occurring at a constant rate in fixed time interval, independently
(ii) Geometric(p=0.5) — number of trials until first success
(iii) Hypergeometric(N=20, K=4, n=5) — sampling without replacement from a finite population with known defective count
(iv) Negative Binomial NB(r=3, p=0.3) — number of failures before the r-th success

questions.title: Poisson and Geometric properties
questions.prompt: For a Poisson(λ) random variable X: (i) state its PMF, mean, and variance; (ii) state one key property that distinguishes it from the Binomial distribution. For a Geometric(p) random variable Y (number of trials until first success): (iii) state its PMF and mean; (iv) state and prove the memoryless property.

markscheme.text: (i) P(X=k) = e^{−λ}λ^k/k! for k=0,1,2,...; E[X]=λ; Var(X)=λ — mean equals variance
(ii) Poisson models rare events in continuous time/space; mean=variance; arises as Binomial limit when n→∞, p→0, np=λ. Binomial has finite n trials; Poisson is unbounded
(iv) Memoryless: P(Y>m+n | Y>m) = P(Y>n). Proof: P(Y>m)=(1−p)^m. Ratio: (1−p)^{m+n}/(1−p)^m = (1−p)^n = P(Y>n) ✓

questions.title: Hypergeometric calculation
questions.prompt:
A standard deck has 52 cards: 13 hearts, 13 spades, 13 diamonds, 13 clubs. You draw 5 cards without replacement. Let X = number of hearts drawn.
(i) State the distribution of X and its parameters.
(ii) Write the PMF formula and compute P(X=2).
(iii) Find E[X].
(iv) In what limit does the Hypergeometric distribution approximate a Binomial, and why?

markscheme.text: (i) X ~ Hypergeometric(N=52, K=13, n=5) — drawing without replacement from population of 52, 13 successes
(iv) When N is very large relative to n (population much larger than sample), sampling without replacement ≈ sampling with replacement. Hypergeometric → Binomial(n, K/N) as N→∞ with K/N fixed

questions.title: Negative Binomial and generating functions
questions.prompt:
(i) Derive the PMF of the Negative Binomial distribution NB(r, p) — the number of failures before the r-th success. Show it generalises the Geometric (r=1).
(ii) Find E[X] for NB(r,p) using the result that X = G₁ + G₂ + ... + Gᵣ where each Gᵢ ~ Geometric₀(p) (number of failures before each successive success). Where Geometric₀ means number of failures, so E[Gᵢ] = (1−p)/p.
(iii) A basketball player makes each free throw independently with probability 0.7. What is the probability that their 5th success comes on the 8th attempt?
questions.hint: For part (iii): need exactly 4 successes in first 7 attempts, then a success on attempt 8.

markscheme.text: (i) NB(r,p): X = number of failures before r-th success. In x+r total trials, need exactly r-1 successes in first x+r-1 trials, then a success. P(X=x) = C(x+r-1, r-1)·p^r·(1−p)^x for x=0,1,2,... For r=1: P(X=x)=p(1−p)^x (Geometric₀) ✓
(ii) X = Σ Gᵢ where Gᵢ ~ Geometric₀(p). E[X] = Σ E[Gᵢ] = r·(1−p)/p (by linearity)

questions.title: Comparing discrete distributions
questions.prompt: This question concerns discrete probability distributions.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: A typist makes typing errors at a rate of 2 per page on average. (i) State the appropriate model and justify it. (ii) Find the probability of no errors on a given page. (iii) Find the probability of more than 3 errors.

markscheme.text: Poisson(λ=2): errors are rare events occurring at a constant average rate, independently. Appropriate when errors don't 'cluster' (each keystroke independent)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A website receives hits according to a Poisson process at rate λ = 5 per minute. (i) What is the probability of at least 3 hits in a 30-second interval? (ii) If hits arrive at rate λ per minute, what is the distribution of hits in t minutes? (iii) Hits arrive from two independent sources at rates λ₁=3 and λ₂=4 per minute. What is the distribution of total hits?

markscheme.text: (ii) Poisson(λt) — scaling property: sum of t independent Poisson(λ) is Poisson(λt)
(iii) Poisson(λ₁+λ₂) = Poisson(7) — sum of independent Poissons is Poisson with summed rates

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: An urn contains 10 balls: 6 red, 4 blue. Three balls are drawn without replacement. Let X = number of red balls drawn. Compute E[X] and Var(X) using the Hypergeometric formulas (E[X] = nK/N, Var(X) = nK(N-K)(N-n) / [N²(N-1)]).

markscheme.text: Compare to Binomial(n=3, p=0.6): E=1.8 (same), Var=3·0.6·0.4=0.72 > 0.56. Hypergeometric has smaller variance (finite population correction factor (N−n)/(N−1) = 7/9 < 1)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Give a real-world scenario modelled by each of the four distributions covered in this topic: Poisson, Geometric, Negative Binomial, Hypergeometric. For each, briefly justify why the distribution applies.

markscheme.text: Poisson: radioactive decay counts per second, customer arrivals per hour, typos per page — events occur independently at constant rate
Geometric: number of job applications until first offer, number of coin flips until first head — independent trials, first success model, memoryless

Negative Binomial: number of sales calls before closing r deals, number of failures before r-th success in clinical trial. Hypergeometric: quality control (defectives in a sample from a batch without replacement), exit polls (number of Labour voters in a sample from a known-proportion electorate)

questions.title: Tripos: Poisson processes and approximations
questions.prompt: This question concerns Poisson approximations and related distributions.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: State precisely the conditions under which Binomial(n,p) is well-approximated by Poisson(λ). Demonstrate the approximation: compute P(X=0) and P(X=2) exactly for Binomial(100, 0.02) and compare to Poisson(2).

markscheme.text: Conditions: n large, p small, np = λ moderate (typically n≥20, p≤0.05)
Formal proof sketch: C(n,k)p^k(1−p)^{n−k} → e^{−λ}λ^k/k! as n→∞, p=λ/n

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A rare disease affects 1 in 500 people. In a sample of 2000 people, what is the probability that exactly 3 are affected? Use the Poisson approximation. State the exact distribution and explain why the approximation is appropriate.

markscheme.text: Exact: Binomial(2000, 1/500) = Binomial(2000, 0.002). λ = np = 4. Poisson(4) is appropriate: n=2000 large, p=0.002 small
Could also use exact Binomial: C(2000,3)·(0.002)³·(0.998)^1997 ≈ same answer to 3 d.p.

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: A Geometric(p) distribution counts the trial number of the first success. Show that the sum of r independent Geometric₀(p) random variables (each counting failures before success) has a Negative Binomial distribution. State the distribution clearly.

markscheme.text: Let X₁,...,Xᵣ be i.i.d. Geometric₀(p) (failures before success). X = X₁+...+Xᵣ = total failures before r-th success
P(X₁=k₁,...,Xᵣ=kᵣ) = ∏ p(1-p)^{kᵢ}. Marginal P(X=x) = C(x+r-1,r-1)p^r(1-p)^x — number of ways to arrange r-1 successes among x+r-1 trials (last is always a success)
This is NB(r,p). E[X] = r(1-p)/p, Var(X) = r(1-p)/p²

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: A Poisson process with rate λ has inter-arrival times that are Exponentially distributed. State this result and briefly explain the connection between the Geometric distribution (discrete memoryless) and the Exponential distribution (continuous memoryless).

markscheme.text: In a Poisson process, inter-arrival times T₁, T₂,... are i.i.d. Exponential(λ). The number of arrivals in [0,t] is Poisson(λt)
Both Geometric and Exponential are the unique memoryless distributions in their respective domains (discrete vs continuous). Geometric: P(X>m+n|X>m)=P(X>n). Exponential: P(T>s+t|T>s)=P(T>t)

Connection: Geometric is the discrete analogue of Exponential. If you discretise a Poisson process by checking at intervals Δt, the waiting time (in discrete steps) is Geometric(p=λΔt). As Δt→0, Geometric→Exponential

questions.title: PDF and CDF basics
questions.prompt: Define the probability density function (PDF) f(x) of a continuous random variable X. What conditions must f satisfy? Define the cumulative distribution function (CDF) F(x) and state how to recover f from F.

markscheme.text: PDF: f(x) ≥ 0 for all x; ∫_{−∞}^{∞} f(x)dx = 1. P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = ∫_a^b f(x)dx
CDF: F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫_{−∞}^x f(t)dt. Properties: non-decreasing, F(−∞)=0, F(∞)=1, right-continuous
Recover PDF from CDF: f(x) = F'(x) wherever the derivative exists. Note: for continuous RVs, P(X=x)=0 for any specific x

questions.title: Uniform and Exponential distributions
questions.prompt: For the Uniform distribution U(a,b): state the PDF, mean, and variance. For the Exponential distribution Exp(λ): state the PDF, mean, variance, and the memoryless property. Explain why the Exponential is the continuous analogue of the Geometric distribution.

markscheme.text: U(a,b): f(x) = 1/(b−a) for a≤x≤b, 0 otherwise. E[X] = (a+b)/2, Var(X) = (b−a)²/12
Exp(λ): f(x) = λe^{−λx} for x≥0. E[X] = 1/λ, Var(X) = 1/λ²
Memoryless: P(X>s+t|X>s) = P(X>t) for all s,t≥0. Proof: P(X>s+t)/P(X>s) = e^{−λ(s+t)}/e^{−λs} = e^{−λt} = P(X>t) ✓

Geometric is the unique discrete memoryless distribution; Exponential is the unique continuous one. Both model waiting time for the first event; the Exponential arises in the continuous-time limit of a Bernoulli process

questions.title: Normal distribution and standardisation
questions.prompt:
The Normal distribution N(μ, σ²) has PDF f(x) = (1/√(2πσ²)) exp(−(x−μ)²/(2σ²)).
(i) State E[X] and Var(X).
(ii) If X ~ N(70, 100), find P(60 < X < 80) using standardisation Z=(X−μ)/σ and the fact that P(−1 < Z < 1) ≈ 0.683.
(iii) If X ~ N(50, 25), find the value c such that P(X ≤ c) = 0.975, given Φ(1.96) = 0.975.
(iv) Why is the Normal distribution central to statistics?
questions.hint: Standardise: Z = (X−μ)/σ ~ N(0,1). Then use the standard normal table.

markscheme.text: (i) E[X] = μ, Var(X) = σ²
(iv) Central Limit Theorem: sums of independent RVs tend to Normal regardless of underlying distribution. Many natural phenomena approximately Normal. Normal is closed under linear combinations

questions.title: Transformations of continuous RVs
questions.prompt:
(i) If X ~ U(0,1), find the PDF of Y = −(1/λ)ln(X) and show Y ~ Exp(λ).
(ii) If X ~ N(0,1), find the PDF of Y = X². (You may use the fact that P(Y ≤ y) = P(−√y ≤ X ≤ √y).)
(iii) State the result: if X ~ Exp(λ), what is P(X > t + s | X > t)? This is used in queueing theory to argue that service times have no 'memory'.
questions.hint: For (i): use the inverse transform method — if F_Y(y) = 1 − e^{−λy}, differentiate to get the PDF.

markscheme.text: (i) F_Y(y) = P(Y≤y) = P(−ln(X)/λ≤y) = P(X≥e^{−λy}) = 1−e^{−λy} for y≥0 → f_Y(y)=λe^{−λy} → Y~Exp(λ) ✓
(ii) F_Y(y) = P(X²≤y) = P(−√y≤X≤√y) = Φ(√y)−Φ(−√y) = 2Φ(√y)−1. f_Y(y)=F'_Y(y) = 2φ(√y)·(1/(2√y)) = φ(√y)/√y = (1/√(2πy))e^{−y/2} for y>0 — this is the chi-squared(1) distribution
(iii) P(X>t+s|X>t) = e^{−λ(t+s)}/e^{−λt} = e^{−λs} = P(X>s). Memoryless: remaining service time has the same distribution regardless of how long service has already taken

questions.title: Continuous distributions: Cambridge Tripos style
questions.prompt: This question concerns continuous random variables.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: A random variable X has PDF f(x) = cx² for 0 ≤ x ≤ 3, and 0 otherwise. (i) Find c. (ii) Find E[X] and Var(X). (iii) Find P(1 < X < 2).

markscheme.text: (i) ∫₀³ cx²dx = c·[x³/3]₀³ = 9c = 1 → c = 1/9

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: The waiting time at a bus stop is uniformly distributed on [0, T] minutes. (i) Find the probability a passenger waits more than T/2. (ii) Find the expected waiting time given the passenger waits more than T/3. (iii) If two passengers arrive independently, find the probability that one waits more than twice as long as the other.

markscheme.text: (i) P(X > T/2) = 1/2 (symmetry of uniform distribution)
(ii) Given X > T/3: X is uniform on (T/3, T). E[X | X > T/3] = (T/3 + T)/2 = 2T/3

(iii) P(X₁ > 2X₂) + P(X₂ > 2X₁) by symmetry = 2P(X₁ > 2X₂). Integrate over unit square: ∫₀^{T/2} ∫₀^{x/2} (1/T²) dy dx = ∫₀^{T/2} x/(2T²) dx = T²/(4·2T²)·... Let T=1: ∫₀^{0.5} x/2 dx = [x²/4]₀^{0.5} = 1/16. Total = 2/16 = 1/8

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: State the Central Limit Theorem for the sample mean. A factory produces items with weight X ~ Exp(1/50) (mean 50g, SD 50g). A box contains 100 items. Use the CLT to approximate P(total weight > 5100g).

markscheme.text: CLT: X̄_n = (X₁+...+X_n)/n → N(μ, σ²/n) in distribution. Sum S_n → N(nμ, nσ²)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: A standard result is that if X ~ N(0,1) and Y ~ N(0,1) independently, then X + Y ~ N(0,2). Verify this using the moment generating function (MGF). State the MGF of N(μ, σ²) and use it.

markscheme.text: MGF of N(μ,σ²): M_X(t) = exp(μt + σ²t²/2). For N(0,1): M(t) = exp(t²/2)
X+Y independent: M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t)·M_Y(t) = exp(t²/2)·exp(t²/2) = exp(t²)
exp(t²) = exp(0·t + 2·t²/2) is the MGF of N(0,2). Since MGFs determine distributions uniquely, X+Y ~ N(0,2) ✓

questions.title: Exponential distribution and applications
questions.prompt: This question concerns the Exponential distribution in applications.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Components in a system have lifetimes T₁, T₂, ..., T_n that are i.i.d. Exp(λ). For a series system (fails when any component fails), find the distribution of the system lifetime T = min(T₁,...,T_n) and state E[T].

markscheme.text: P(T > t) = P(T₁>t, ..., T_n>t) = P(T₁>t)^n = e^{−nλt} (by independence)
T ~ Exp(nλ) — the minimum of n i.i.d. Exp(λ) is Exp(nλ)
E[T] = 1/(nλ) — the more components, the shorter the expected system lifetime

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: For a parallel system (fails when all components fail), find P(T_sys > t) where T_sys = max(T₁,...,T_n). Derive E[T_sys] for n=2 using E[max(T₁,T₂)] = E[T₁] + E[T₂] − E[min(T₁,T₂)].

markscheme.text: P(T_sys > t) = 1 − P(T_sys ≤ t) = 1 − P(T₁≤t)^n = 1 − (1−e^{−λt})^n
E[max(T₁,T₂)] = E[T₁]+E[T₂]−E[min(T₁,T₂)] = 1/λ + 1/λ − 1/(2λ) = 3/(2λ)
With 2 parallel components, expected lifetime = 3/(2λ) > 1/λ (single component) — redundancy improves reliability

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: A random variable X has CDF F(x). If U ~ U(0,1), show that X = F^{−1}(U) has CDF F (the inverse transform method). Why is this useful for simulation?

markscheme.text: P(F^{−1}(U) ≤ x) = P(U ≤ F(x)) = F(x) (since U ~ U(0,1) and F is non-decreasing, F(U) ≤ F(x) iff U ≤ F(x))
Simulation: can generate X from any distribution by (1) generating U ~ U(0,1), then (2) computing X = F^{−1}(U). Only need to implement F^{−1}. Used to simulate Exponential: X = −(1/λ)log(U)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Why does the Normal distribution arise so frequently in nature? Give two examples from different fields and explain the underlying mechanism via the CLT.

markscheme.text: CLT: sum/average of many independent random variables (even non-Normal ones) converges to Normal. Appears whenever a quantity is influenced by many small independent factors

Example 1: human height — determined by many genetic and environmental factors, each contributing a small random increment → approximately Normal. Example 2: measurement errors — sum of many small instrument and human errors → Normal (motivation for Gauss's method of least squares)
Also: financial returns (sum of many small price changes), thermal noise in electronics (sum of many electron collisions). The key assumption is independence and similar magnitudes of contributions

questions.title: Joint distribution definitions
questions.prompt: Define the joint PMF for two discrete random variables X and Y. Define the marginal PMF of X. State the relationship between the joint PMF and the marginals. Give a 2×2 example joint distribution table.

markscheme.text: Joint PMF: p_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X=x, Y=y). Must satisfy: p(x,y) ≥ 0 and Σ_{x,y} p(x,y) = 1
Marginal PMF of X: p_X(x) = Σ_y p_{X,Y}(x,y). Marginalize by summing over the other variable (marginalise out Y)

questions.title: Joint to marginal and conditional
questions.prompt:
Two discrete random variables X (values 1,2) and Y (values 1,2,3) have joint PMF given by: p(1,1)=0.1, p(1,2)=0.2, p(1,3)=0.1, p(2,1)=0.2, p(2,2)=0.3, p(2,3)=0.1.
(i) Find the marginal PMFs of X and Y.
(ii) Find P(X=2 | Y=2).
(iii) Are X and Y independent? Justify briefly.

markscheme.text: Check independence: P(X=1,Y=1)=0.1; P(X=1)·P(Y=1) = 0.4×0.3 = 0.12 ≠ 0.1 → NOT independent

questions.title: Continuous joint distribution
questions.prompt:
Two continuous random variables X and Y have joint PDF f(x,y) = 6xy for 0 ≤ x ≤ 1, 0 ≤ y ≤ 1 (and 0 otherwise, but note this doesn't integrate to 1 — use f(x,y) = 6x for 0≤x≤1, 0≤y≤x instead).
Actually use: f(x,y) = 2 for 0 ≤ y ≤ x ≤ 1. Find: (i) the marginal PDF of X; (ii) the marginal PDF of Y; (iii) P(X > 0.5).
questions.hint: For the marginal of X: integrate over the range of y (0 to x). For the marginal of Y: integrate over the range of x (y to 1).

markscheme.text: (i) f_X(x) = ∫₀^x 2 dy = 2x for 0≤x≤1. Verify: ∫₀¹ 2x dx = 1 ✓
(ii) f_Y(y) = ∫_y^1 2 dx = 2(1−y) for 0≤y≤1. Verify: ∫₀¹ 2(1−y) dy = 1 ✓

questions.title: Conditional distributions and expectations
questions.prompt:
Given the joint PDF f(x,y) = 2 for 0≤y≤x≤1 from above:
(i) Find the conditional PDF of Y given X=x.
(ii) Find E[Y | X=x].
(iii) Using the tower property E[Y] = E[E[Y|X]], verify that E[Y] = 1/3.
(iv) Check by computing E[Y] directly from the marginal f_Y(y) = 2(1−y).

markscheme.text: (i) f_{Y|X=x}(y) = f(x,y)/f_X(x) = 2/(2x) = 1/x for 0≤y≤x. This is U(0,x)
(ii) E[Y|X=x] = x/2 (mean of U(0,x))

questions.title: Joint distributions: Tripos style
questions.prompt: This question concerns joint distributions of two random variables.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: X and Y are jointly distributed with joint PMF p(x,y) = c(x+y) for x ∈ {1,2} and y ∈ {1,2}. (i) Find c. (ii) Find marginals P_X and P_Y. (iii) Are X and Y independent?

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: X ~ U(0,1) and Y = X² (deterministic function of X). (i) Find the CDF and PDF of Y. (ii) Find E[Y] two ways: via the PDF of Y and via LOTUS. (iii) Does the pair (X,Y) have a joint PDF? Explain.

markscheme.text: (i) F_Y(y) = P(X²≤y) = P(X≤√y) = √y for 0≤y≤1. f_Y(y) = 1/(2√y) for 0<y≤1
(ii) Via f_Y: E[Y]=∫₀¹ y·(1/(2√y))dy = ∫₀¹ √y/2 dy = [y^{3/2}/3]₀¹ = 1/3. Via LOTUS: E[X²]=∫₀¹ x²dx = 1/3 ✓
(iii) No — (X,Y) does not have a joint PDF. All probability mass lies on the curve y=x² in ℝ², which has measure zero. The pair is perfectly dependent (Y is a function of X)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: The joint PDF of (X,Y) is f(x,y) = e^{−x−y} for x,y ≥ 0. (i) Show X and Y are independent. (ii) Find P(X+Y ≤ 1).

markscheme.text: (i) f(x,y) = e^{−x}·e^{−y} = f_X(x)·f_Y(y) where f_X(x)=e^{−x} and f_Y(y)=e^{−y} (both Exp(1)). Joint density factorises → independent
(ii) P(X+Y≤1) = ∫₀¹∫₀^{1−x} e^{−x−y} dy dx. Inner: ∫₀^{1−x} e^{−y}dy = 1−e^{−(1−x)}

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Define the joint CDF F(a,b) = P(X≤a, Y≤b). How do you recover the joint PDF from F? What is the marginal CDF F_X(a) in terms of F?

markscheme.text: f(x,y) = ∂²F/∂x∂y (mixed second partial derivative, where it exists)
F_X(a) = P(X≤a) = P(X≤a, Y<∞) = lim_{b→∞} F(a,b) = F(a,∞)
Similarly F_Y(b) = F(∞,b). Independence iff F(a,b) = F_X(a)·F_Y(b) for all a,b

questions.title: Bivariate Normal and sums of RVs
questions.prompt: This question concerns distributions of sums of random variables.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: If X ~ N(μ₁,σ₁²) and Y ~ N(μ₂,σ₂²) are independent, what is the distribution of X+Y? State the result and briefly justify using moment generating functions.

markscheme.text: X+Y ~ N(μ₁+μ₂, σ₁²+σ₂²)
MGF of X: e^{μ₁t+σ₁²t²/2}. MGF of Y: e^{μ₂t+σ₂²t²/2}. Product: e^{(μ₁+μ₂)t+(σ₁²+σ₂²)t²/2}
This is the MGF of N(μ₁+μ₂, σ₁²+σ₂²). MGFs uniquely determine distributions → result follows

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: The convolution formula: for independent X and Y with PDFs f_X and f_Y, show that the PDF of Z=X+Y is f_Z(z) = ∫ f_X(x)f_Y(z−x)dx. Apply this to find the PDF of the sum of two independent U(0,1) random variables.

markscheme.text: F_Z(z)=P(X+Y≤z)=∫∫_{x+y≤z}f_X(x)f_Y(y)dxdy=∫f_X(x)(∫_{−∞}^{z−x}f_Y(y)dy)dx=∫f_X(x)F_Y(z−x)dx. Differentiate: f_Z = ∫f_X(x)f_Y(z−x)dx
U(0,1)+U(0,1): f_Z(z)=∫₀¹ f_X(x)·𝟏_{0≤z−x≤1}dx. For 0≤z≤1: integrate x from 0 to z → f_Z(z)=z
For 1<z≤2: integrate x from z−1 to 1 → f_Z(z)=2−z. Triangular distribution on [0,2]

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: In a portfolio, two stock returns X ~ N(0.05, 0.01) and Y ~ N(0.03, 0.04) have correlation ρ = 0.3. The portfolio return is R = 0.5X + 0.5Y. Find E[R] and Var(R).

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: State the law of total expectation and law of total variance. Use them to find E[Y] and Var(Y) where Y|X=x ~ Poisson(x) and X ~ Exp(1).

markscheme.text: E[Y] = E[E[Y|X]] = E[X] = 1/1 = 1 (since E[Y|X=x]=x and E[X]=1)
Law of total variance: Var(Y) = E[Var(Y|X)] + Var(E[Y|X]) = E[X] + Var(X) (since Var(Poisson(x))=x, Var(X)=1/λ²=1)

questions.title: Covariance definition and formula
questions.prompt: Define Cov(X,Y). State the alternative formula Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y]. What does a positive/negative/zero covariance imply? What is Cov(X,X)?

markscheme.text: Cov(X,Y) = E[(X−E[X])(Y−E[Y])]. Alternative: Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y]
Positive: X above mean tends to coincide with Y above mean (they move together). Negative: opposite directions. Zero: uncorrelated (no linear relationship)
Cov(X,X) = E[(X−E[X])²] = Var(X)

questions.title: Correlation coefficient and its bounds
questions.prompt: (i) Define the correlation coefficient ρ(X,Y) and state why it is preferred over covariance for comparing relationships. (ii) Prove that ρ(X,Y) ≥ −1 using the fact that Var(X/σ_X + Y/σ_Y) ≥ 0. (iii) State the condition under which ρ = 1 or ρ = −1.

markscheme.text: ρ(X,Y) = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y). Preferred: dimensionless and scale-invariant — ρ(αX, βY) = ρ(X,Y) for α,β>0
0 ≤ Var(X/σ_X + Y/σ_Y) = 1 + 1 + 2ρ = 2(1+ρ) → ρ ≥ −1. Similarly 0 ≤ Var(X/σ_X − Y/σ_Y) = 2(1−ρ) → ρ ≤ 1
ρ=1 iff Y = aX+b with a>0 (perfect positive linear relationship). ρ=−1 iff Y = aX+b with a<0

questions.title: Independence vs uncorrelated: counterexample
questions.prompt:
(i) Prove that if X and Y are independent, then Cov(X,Y) = 0 (discrete case).
(ii) Show the converse is false using the counterexample X uniform on {−1,0,1} and Y = 1 if X=0, Y=0 otherwise. Show Cov(X,Y)=0 but X,Y are not independent.
(iii) What does this tell us about the limitations of covariance/correlation?

markscheme.text: (i) E[XY] = Σ_{x,y} xy·P(X=x,Y=y) = Σ_{x,y} xy·P(X=x)P(Y=y) = (Σ_x x·P(X=x))(Σ_y y·P(Y=y)) = E[X]E[Y] → Cov=0
(ii) XY=0 always (either X=0 giving Y=1 but XY=0, or X≠0 giving Y=0). E[XY]=0. E[X]=0 by symmetry. Cov=0−0=0. But P(X=0,Y=1)=1/3 while P(X=0)P(Y=1)=(1/3)(1/3)=1/9≠1/3. Not independent
(iii) Covariance/correlation only detects linear dependence. Nonlinear (or higher-order) dependence — where Y depends on X but via a nonlinear function — is invisible to ρ

questions.title: Variance of sums and the Buffon's Needle problem
questions.prompt:
(i) State the general formula for Var(X+Y). Use it to show that if X ~ Bernoulli(p) and Y ~ Bernoulli(q) are independent and Z = X+Y, then Var(Z) = p(1-p) + q(1-q).
(ii) Buffon's Needle: a needle of length L ≤ D is dropped randomly on a floor with parallel lines distance D apart. Let X be the distance from the needle's centre to the nearest line (X ~ U[0,D/2]) and θ be the angle with the lines (θ ~ U[0,π/2]), independent. Show P(needle crosses a line) = 2L/(πD).
State how this could estimate π.
questions.hint: For (ii): the needle crosses a line iff X < (L/2)cos(θ). Integrate the joint density over the crossing region.

markscheme.text: (i) Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y)+2Cov(X,Y). Independent → Cov=0 → Var(Z)=p(1-p)+q(1-q)
(ii) Joint density: f_X(x)=2/D on [0,D/2], f_θ(θ)=2/π on [0,π/2]. Joint: f(x,θ)=4/(πD)
Estimate π: throw n needles, count k crossings. p̂ = k/n ≈ 2L/(πD) → π ≈ 2Ln/(kD). This is a Monte Carlo method

questions.title: Covariance in portfolio and random sums
questions.prompt: This question concerns covariance and its applications.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Prove the bilinearity of covariance: (i) Cov(aX+b, Y) = a·Cov(X,Y); (ii) Cov(X+Y, Z) = Cov(X,Z)+Cov(Y,Z). Use these to show Var(X−Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y)−2Cov(X,Y).

markscheme.text: (i) Cov(aX+b,Y) = E[(aX+b−E[aX+b])(Y−E[Y])] = E[a(X−E[X])(Y−E[Y])] = a·Cov(X,Y)
(ii) Cov(X+Y,Z) = E[(X+Y−E[X+Y])(Z−E[Z])] = E[(X−E[X]+Y−E[Y])(Z−E[Z])] = Cov(X,Z)+Cov(Y,Z)
Var(X−Y) = Cov(X−Y,X−Y) = Cov(X,X)−Cov(X,Y)−Cov(Y,X)+Cov(Y,Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y)−2Cov(X,Y)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A portfolio consists of n stocks with returns X₁,...,Xₙ, each with mean μ and variance σ², and pairwise covariance ρσ² (correlation ρ). The portfolio return is R = (1/n)∑Xᵢ. Find E[R] and Var(R). What happens as n→∞ for ρ>0?

markscheme.text: E[R] = μ (by linearity)
Var(R) = (1/n²)[n·σ² + n(n−1)·ρσ²] = σ²/n + (n−1)ρσ²/n = σ²(1−ρ)/n + ρσ²
As n→∞: Var(R) → ρσ². The 'systematic risk' ρσ² cannot be diversified away; only the 'idiosyncratic risk' (1−ρ)σ²/n vanishes. Key insight in Modern Portfolio Theory

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: X₁,...,Xₙ are i.i.d. Bernoulli(p). Define S = X₁+...+Xₙ. (i) Compute Cov(Xᵢ, S). (ii) Compute Corr(Xᵢ, S) = ρ(Xᵢ,S). (iii) Does ρ(Xᵢ,S) increase or decrease as n increases? Interpret.

markscheme.text: (i) Cov(Xᵢ,S) = Cov(Xᵢ, Xᵢ+Σ_{j≠i}Xⱼ) = Var(Xᵢ) + 0 = p(1−p) (by independence of Xⱼ for j≠i)
(iii) Decreases as n grows (1/√n→0). Each individual component becomes less correlated with the total as n grows — consistent with law of large numbers

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: State Cauchy-Schwarz inequality for random variables: |E[XY]|² ≤ E[X²]E[Y²]. Use it to prove |ρ(X,Y)| ≤ 1 directly.

markscheme.text: Cauchy-Schwarz: for any RVs X,Y, |E[XY]|² ≤ E[X²]E[Y²]
Apply to centred variables X−μ_X and Y−μ_Y: |Cov(X,Y)|² = |E[(X−μ_X)(Y−μ_Y)]|² ≤ E[(X−μ_X)²]E[(Y−μ_Y)²] = Var(X)Var(Y)
|Cov(X,Y)| ≤ √(Var(X))√(Var(Y)) = σ_X σ_Y → |ρ| = |Cov(X,Y)|/(σ_X σ_Y) ≤ 1 ✓. Equality iff Y−μ_Y = c(X−μ_X) a.s. (perfect linear relationship)

questions.title: Tripos: Buffon, covariance structure, and CLT
questions.prompt: This question concerns independence and correlation.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Recall Buffon's needle: P(crossing) = 2L/(πD). An experiment drops n=1000 needles (L=D=1) and observes k crossings. Estimate π. Find the standard error of your π estimate in terms of n and the true crossing probability p=2/π.

markscheme.text: p̂ = k/n. π estimate: π̂ = 2/p̂ = 2n/k
By delta method: Var(π̂) ≈ (dπ̂/dp)²·Var(p̂). dπ̂/dp = −2/p². Var(p̂)=p(1−p)/n

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Two random variables X and Y are said to be conditionally independent given Z if P(X∈A, Y∈B | Z=z) = P(X∈A|Z=z)P(Y∈B|Z=z) for all A,B,z. Give an example where X and Y are (i) independent but not conditionally independent given Z; (ii) dependent but conditionally independent given Z.

markscheme.text: (i) X=fair coin flip, Y=fair coin flip (independent). Z=X⊕Y (XOR). Given Z=0, knowing X determines Y → not conditionally independent given Z

(ii) X=symptom A, Y=symptom B (both caused by disease Z). Marginally X and Y are correlated (both more common when sick). Given Z (disease status known), X and Y become independent (naive Bayes assumption). This is the hidden common cause structure

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: The sample variance S² = (1/(n-1))Σ(Xᵢ − X̄)² is an unbiased estimator of σ². (i) Compute E[Σ(Xᵢ−X̄)²] by writing Xᵢ−X̄ = (Xᵢ−μ)−(X̄−μ). (ii) Why is the denominator n-1 rather than n?

markscheme.text: Σ(Xᵢ−X̄)² = Σ(Xᵢ−μ)² − n(X̄−μ)². E[Σ(Xᵢ−μ)²]=nσ². E[n(X̄−μ)²]=n·Var(X̄)=n·σ²/n=σ²
E[Σ(Xᵢ−X̄)²] = nσ²−σ² = (n-1)σ²
Denominator n-1: ensures E[S²]=σ² (unbiasedness). Using n would give biased estimator. The n-1 corrects for 'losing one degree of freedom' by estimating μ with X̄

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: The correlation coefficient ρ̂ = Σ(Xᵢ−X̄)(Yᵢ−Ȳ) / √(Σ(Xᵢ−X̄)²·Σ(Yᵢ−Ȳ)²) is the sample correlation. (i) Why is −1 ≤ ρ̂ ≤ 1? (ii) A study finds ρ̂ = 0.9 between ice cream sales and drowning rates. Is there a causal relationship? (iii) What statistical concept explains this?

markscheme.text: (i) By Cauchy-Schwarz on vectors: |Σ(Xᵢ−X̄)(Yᵢ−Ȳ)| ≤ √(Σ(Xᵢ−X̄)²)·√(Σ(Yᵢ−Ȳ)²) → |ρ̂| ≤ 1
(ii) No causal relationship — correlation does not imply causation

(iii) Confounding variable: hot weather (summer) causes both more ice cream sales AND more swimming → more drownings. The common cause (temperature/season) creates apparent correlation between two otherwise unrelated variables
